rows.pali: Yathāha –"ratto kho, brāhmaṇa, rāgena abhibhūto pariyādiṇṇacitto attabyābādhāyapi ceteti, parabyābādhāyapi ceteti, ubhayabyābādhāyapi ceteti.
rows.english: according as it is said: “When a man is dyed with greed, brahman, and is overwhelmed and his mind is obsessed by greed, then he thinks for his own affliction, he thinks for others’ affliction, he thinks for the affliction of both,

rows.pali: Cetasikaṃ dukkhaṃ domanassaṃ paṭisaṃvedeti.
rows.english: and he experiences mental suffering and grief.

rows.pali: Rāge pahīne neva attabyābādhāya ceteti, na parabyābādhāya ceteti, na ubhayabyābādhāya ceteti, na cetasikaṃ dukkhaṃ domanassaṃ paṭisaṃvedeti.
rows.english: When greed has been abandoned, he neither thinks for his own affliction, nor thinks for others’ affliction, nor thinks for the affliction of both, and he does not experience mental suffering and grief.

rows.pali: Evampi kho, brāhmaṇa, sandiṭṭhiko dhammo hotī"ti(a. ni. 3.54).
rows.english: This, brahman, is how the Dhamma is visible here and now” (A I 156). [216]

rows.pali: Apica navavidhopi lokuttaradhammo yena yena adhigato hoti, tena tena parasaddhāya gantabbataṃ hitvā paccavekkhaṇañāṇena sayaṃ daṭṭhabboti sandiṭṭhiko.
rows.english: 77.Furthermore, the ninefold supramundane Dhamma is also visible here and now, since when anyone has attained it, it is visible to him through reviewing knowledge without his having to rely on faith in another.

rows.pali: Atha vā pasatthā diṭṭhi sandiṭṭhi, sandiṭṭhiyā jayatīti sandiṭṭhiko.
rows.english: 78.Or alternatively, the view (diṭṭhi) that is recommended (pasattha—pp. of root saṃs) is “proper view” (sandiṭṭhi). It conquers by means of proper view, thus it “has proper view” (sandiṭṭhika—“visible here and now”).

rows.pali: Tathā hettha ariyamaggo sampayuttāya, ariyaphalaṃ kāraṇabhūtāya, nibbānaṃ visayibhūtāya sandiṭṭhiyā kilese jayati.
rows.english: For in this way the noble path conquers defilements by means of the proper view associated with it, and the noble fruition does so by means of the proper view that is its cause, and Nibbāna does so by means of the proper view that has Nibbāna as its objective field.

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā rathena jayatīti rathiko, evaṃ navavidhopi lokuttaradhammo sandiṭṭhiyā jayatīti sandiṭṭhiko.
rows.english: So the ninefold supramundane Dhamma “has the proper view” (sandiṭṭhika—“is visible here and now”) since it conquers by means of proper view, just as a charioteer (rathika) is so called because he conquers by means of a chariot (ratha).

rows.pali: Atha vā diṭṭhanti dassanaṃ vuccati.
rows.english: 79. Or alternatively, it is seeing (dassana) that is called “the seen” (diṭṭha);

rows.pali: Diṭṭhameva sandiṭṭhaṃ, dassananti attho.
rows.english: then diṭṭha and sandiṭṭha are identical in meaning as “seeing”.

rows.pali: Sandiṭṭhaṃ arahatīti sandiṭṭhiko.
rows.english: It is worthy of being seen (diṭṭha), thus it is “visible here and now” (sandiṭṭhika).

rows.pali: Lokuttaradhammo hi bhāvanābhisamayavasena sacchikiriyābhisamayavasena ca dissamānoyeva vaṭṭabhayaṃ nivatteti.
rows.english: For the supramundane Dhamma (law) arrests the fearful round [of kamma, etc.,] as soon as it is seen by means of penetration consisting in development [of the path] and by means of penetration consisting in realization [of Nibbāna].

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā vatthaṃ arahatīti vatthiko, evaṃ sandiṭṭhaṃ arahatīti sandiṭṭhiko.
rows.english: So it is “visible here and now” (sandiṭṭhika) since it is worthy of being seen (diṭṭha), just as one who is clothable (vattihika)33 is so called because he is worthy of clothes (vattha).

rows.pali: 149.Attano phaladānaṃ sandhāya nāssa kāloti akālo.
rows.english: 80.It has no delay (lit. “takes no time”—kāla) in the matter of giving its own fruit, thus it is “without delay” (akāla).

rows.pali: Akāloyeva akāliko.
rows.english: “Without delay” is the same as “not delayed” (akālika).

rows.pali: Na pañcāhasattāhādibhedaṃ kālaṃ khepetvā phalaṃ deti, attano pana pavattisamanantarameva phaladoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: What is meant is that instead of giving its fruit after creating a delay (using up time), say, five days, seven days, it gives its fruit immediately next to its own occurrence (see Sn 226).

rows.pali: Atha vā attano phaladāne pakaṭṭho kālo patto assāti kāliko.
rows.english: 81. Or alternatively, what is delayed (kālika—lit. “what takes time”) is what needs some distant34 time to be reached before it can give its fruit.

rows.pali: Ko so?
rows.english: What is that?

rows.pali: Lokiyo kusaladhammo.
rows.english: It is the mundane law of profitable [kamma].

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana samanantaraphalattā na kālikoti akāliko.
rows.english: This, however, is undelayed (na kālika) because its fruit comes immediately next to it, so it is “not delayed” (akālika).

rows.pali: Idaṃ maggameva sandhāya vuttaṃ.
rows.english: This is said with reference to the path.

rows.pali: 150."Ehi passa imaṃ dhamma"nti evaṃ pavattaṃ ehipassavidhiṃ arahatīti ehipassiko.
rows.english: 82.It is worthy of an invitation to inspect (ehipassa-vidhi) given thus: “Come and see this Dhamma” (ehi passa imaṃ dhammaṃ), thus it is “inviting of inspection” (ehipassika).

rows.pali: Kasmā panesa taṃ vidhiṃ arahatīti?
rows.english: But why is it worthy of this invitation?

rows.pali: Vijjamānattā parisuddhattā ca.
rows.english: Because it is found and because of its purity.

rows.pali: Rittamuṭṭhiyaṃ hi hiraññaṃ vā suvaṇṇaṃ vā atthīti vatvāpi "ehi passa ima"nti na sakkā vattuṃ.
rows.english: For if a man has said that there is money or gold in an empty fist, he cannot say, “Come and see it.”

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why not?

rows.pali: Avijjamānattā.
rows.english: Because it is not found.

rows.pali: Vijjamānampi ca gūthaṃ vā muttaṃ vā manuññabhāvappakāsanena cittasampahaṃsanatthaṃ "ehi passa ima"nti na sakkā vattuṃ.
rows.english: And on the other hand, while dung or urine may well be found, a man cannot, for the purpose of cheering the mind by exhibiting beauty, say, “Come and see this;”

rows.pali: Apica kho pana tiṇehi vā paṇṇehi vā paṭicchādetabbameva hoti.
rows.english: on the contrary, they have to be covered up with grass and leaves.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Aparisuddhattā.
rows.english: Because of their impurity.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana navavidhopi lokuttaradhammo sabhāvatova vijjamāno vigatavalāhake ākāse sampuṇṇacandamaṇḍalaṃ viya paṇḍukambale nikkhittajātimaṇi viya ca parisuddho.
rows.english: But this ninefold supramundane Dhamma is actually found as such in its individual essence, and it is as pure as the full moon’s disk in a cloudless sky, as a gem of pure water on bleached cloth.

rows.pali: Tasmā vijjamānattā parisuddhattā ca ehipassavidhiṃ arahatīti ehipassiko.
rows.english: [217] Consequently, it is worthy of the invitation to inspect since it is found and pure, thus it is “inviting of inspection. ”

rows.pali: 151.Upanetabboti opaneyyiko.
rows.english: 83. The word opanayika (“onward-leading”) is [equivalent to the gerund] upanetabba (“ought to—can—be induced”).

rows.pali: Ayaṃ panettha vinicchayo, upanayanaṃ upanayo, ādittaṃ celaṃ vā sīsaṃ vā ajjhupekkhitvāpi bhāvanāvasena attano citte upanayanaṃ arahatīti opanayiko.
rows.english: Here is an exposition. An inducing (upanayana) is an inducement (upanaya). [As the four paths and four fruitions] this [Dhamma] is worth inducing (upanayanaṃ arahati) [that is, arousing] in one’s own mind [subjectively] by means of development, without any question of whether or not one’s clothing or one’s head is on fire (see A IV 320),

rows.pali: Opanayikova opaneyyiko.
rows.english: thus it is “onward-leading” (opanayika).

rows.pali: Idaṃ saṅkhate lokuttaradhamme yujjati.
rows.english: This applies to the [above-mentioned eight] formed supramundane states (dhammas).

rows.pali: Asaṅkhate pana attano cittena upanayanaṃ arahatīti opaneyyiko.
rows.english: But the unformed [dhamma] is worth inducing by one’s own mind [to become the mind’s object], thus it is “onward- leading,” too;

rows.pali: Sacchikiriyāvasena allīyanaṃ arahatīti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is that it is worth treating as one’s shelter by realizing it.

rows.pali: Atha vā nibbānaṃ upanetīti ariyamaggo upaneyyo.
rows.english: 84. Or alternatively, what induces (upaneti) [the noble person] onwards to Nibbāna is the noble path, which is thus inductive (upaneyya).

rows.pali: Sacchikātabbataṃ upanetabboti phalanibbānadhammo upaneyyo.
rows.english: Again, what can (ought to) be induced (upanetabba) to realizability is the Dhamma consisting in fruition and Nibbāna, which is thus inductive (upaneyya), too.

rows.pali: Upaneyyo eva opaneyyiko.
rows.english: The word upaneyya is the same as the word opanayika. 35

rows.pali: 152.Paccattaṃ veditabbo viññūhīti sabbehipi ugghaṭitaññūādīhi viññūhi attani attani veditabbo "bhāvito me maggo, adhigataṃ phalaṃ, sacchikato nirodho"ti.
rows.english: 85.Is directly experienceable by the wise: it can be experienced by all the kinds of wise men beginning with the “acutely wise” (see A II 135) each in himself thus: “The path has been developed, fruition attained, and cessation realized, by me.”

rows.pali: Na hi upajjhāyena bhāvitena maggena saddhivihārikassa kilesā pahīyanti, na so tassa phalasamāpattiyā phāsuviharati, na tena sacchikataṃ nibbānaṃ sacchikaroti.
rows.english: For it does not happen that when a preceptor has developed the path his co- resident abandons his defilements, nor does a co-resident dwell in comfort owing to the preceptor’s attainment of fruition, nor does he realize the Nibbāna realized by the preceptor.

rows.pali: Tasmā na esa parassa sīse ābharaṇaṃ viya daṭṭhabbo, attano pana citteyeva daṭṭhabbo, anubhavitabbo viññūhīti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: So this is not visible in the way that an ornament on another’s head is, but rather it is visible only in one’s own mind.

rows.pali: Bālānaṃ pana avisayo cesa.
rows.english: What is meant is that it can be undergone by wise men, but it is not the province of fools.

rows.pali: Apica svākkhāto ayaṃ dhammo.
rows.english: 86. Now, in addition, this Dhamma is well proclaimed.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Sandiṭṭhikattā.
rows.english: Because it is visible here and now.

rows.pali: Sandiṭṭhiko, akālikattā.
rows.english: It is visible here and now because it is not delayed.

rows.pali: Akāliko, ehipassikattā.
rows.english: It is not delayed because it invites inspection.

rows.pali: Yo ca ehipassiko, so nāma opaneyyiko hotīti.
rows.english: And what invites inspection is onward- leading.

rows.pali: 153.Tassevaṃ svākkhātatādibhede dhammaguṇe anussarato neva tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 87.As long as [the meditator] recollects the special qualities of the Dhamma in this way, then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed,

rows.pali: Na dosa - pe - na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: or obsessed by hate, or obsessed by delusion;

rows.pali: Ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti dhammaṃ ārabbhāti (a. ni. 6.10) purimanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by the Dhamma” (A III 285). So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already described (§66), the jhāna factors arise in a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Dhammaguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāraguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the Dhamma’s special qualities, or else owing to his being occupied in recollecting special qualities of many sorts, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ dhammaguṇānussaraṇavasena uppannattā dhammānussaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access jhāna itself is known as “recollection of the Dhamma” too because it arises with the recollection of the Dhamma’s special qualities as the means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana dhammānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu evaṃ opaneyyikassa dhammassa desetāraṃ imināpaṅgena samannāgataṃ satthāraṃ neva atītaṃse samanupassāmi, na panetarahi aññatra tena bhagavatāti evaṃ dhammaguṇadassaneneva satthari sagāravo hoti sappatisso.
rows.english: 88. [218] When a bhikkhu is devoted to this recollection of the Dhamma, he thinks: “I never in the past met a master who taught a law that led onward thus, who possessed this talent, nor do I now see any such a master other than the Blessed One. ” Seeing the Dhamma’s special qualities in this way, he is respectful and deferential towards the Master.

rows.pali: Dhamme garucittīkāro saddhādivepullaṃ adhigacchati, pītipāmojjabahulo hoti, bhayabheravasaho, dukkhādhivāsanasamattho, dhammena saṃvāsasaññaṃ paṭilabhati, dhammaguṇānussatiyā ajjhāvutthañcassa sarīrampi cetiyagharamiva pūjārahaṃ hoti, anuttaradhammādhigamāya cittaṃ namati, vītikkamitabbavatthusamāyoge cassa dhammasudhammataṃ samanussarato hirottappaṃ paccupaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: He entertains great reverence for the Dhamma and attains fullness of faith, and so on. He has much happiness and gladness. He conquers fear and dread. He is able to endure pain. He comes to feel as if he were living in the Dhamma’s presence. And his body, when the recollection of the Dhamma’s special qualities dwells in it, becomes as worthy of veneration as a shrine room. His mind tends towards the realization of the peerless Dhamma. When he encounters an opportunity for transgression, he has vivid awareness of conscience and shame on recollecting the well-regulatedness of the Dhamma.

rows.pali: Uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ dhammānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of the Dhamma in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 154.Saṅghānussatiṃ bhāvetukāmenāpi rahogatena paṭisallīnena "suppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, ujuppaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, ñāyappaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, sāmīcippaṭipanno bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, yadidaṃ cattāri purisayugāni aṭṭha purisapuggalā, esa bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho āhuneyyo, pāhuneyyo, dakkhiṇeyyo, añjalikaraṇīyo, anuttaraṃ puññakkhettaṃ lokassā"ti (a. ni. 6.10) evaṃ ariyasaṅghaguṇā anussaritabbā.
rows.english: 89.One who wants to develop the recollection of the Community should go into solitary retreat and recollect the special qualities of the community of Noble Ones as follows: “The community of the Blessed One’s disciples has entered on the good way, the community of the Blessed One’s disciples has entered on the straight way, the community of the Blessed One’s disciples has entered on the true way, the community of the Blessed One’s disciples has entered on the proper way, that is to say, the four pairs of men, the eight persons; this community of the Blessed One’s disciples is fit for gifts, fit for hospitality, fit for offerings, fit for reverential salutation, as an incomparable field of merit for the world” (A III 286).

rows.pali: 155.Tattha suppaṭipannoti suṭṭhu paṭipanno, sammāpaṭipadaṃ anivattipaṭipadaṃ anulomapaṭipadaṃ apaccanīkapaṭipadaṃ dhammānudhammapaṭipadaṃ paṭipannoti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 90. Herein, entered on the good way (supaṭipanna) is thoroughly entered on the way (suṭṭhu paṭipanna). What is meant is that it has entered on a way (paṭipanna) that is the right way (sammā-paṭipadā), the way that is irreversible, the way that is in conformity [with truth], the way that has no opposition, the way that is regulated by the Dhamma.

rows.pali: Bhagavato ovādānusāsaniṃ sakkaccaṃ suṇantīti sāvakā.
rows.english: They hear (suṇanti) attentively the Blessed One’s instruction, thus they are his disciples (sāvaka—lit. “hearers”).

rows.pali: Sāvakānaṃ saṅgho sāvakasaṅgho, sīladiṭṭhisāmaññatāya saṅghātabhāvamāpanno sāvakasamūhoti attho.
rows.english: The community of the disciples is the community of those disciples. The meaning is that the total of disciples forms a communality because it possesses in common both virtue and [right] view.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana sā sammāpaṭipadā uju avaṅkā akuṭilā ajimhā, ariyo ca ñāyotipi vuccati, anucchavikattā ca sāmīcītipi saṅkhaṃ gatā.
rows.english: [219] That right way, being straight, unbent, uncrooked, unwarped, is called noble and true and is known as proper owing to its becomingness,

rows.pali: Tasmā tampaṭipanno ariyasaṅgho ujuppaṭipanno ñāyappaṭipanno sāmīcippaṭipannotipi vutto.
rows.english: therefore the noble community that has entered on that is also said to have entered on the straight way, entered on the true way, and entered on the proper way.

rows.pali: Ettha ca ye maggaṭṭhā, te sammāpaṭipattisamaṅgitāya suppaṭipannā.
rows.english: 91.Those who stand on the path can be understood to have entered on the good way since they possess the right way.

rows.pali: Ye phalaṭṭhā, te sammāpaṭipadāya adhigantabbassa adhigatattā atītaṃ paṭipadaṃ sandhāya suppaṭipannāti veditabbā.
rows.english: And those who stand in fruition can be understood to have entered on the good way with respect to the way that is now past since by means of the right way they have realized what should be realized.

rows.pali: Apica svākkhāte dhammavinaye yathānusiṭṭhaṃ paṭipannattāpi apaṇṇakapaṭipadaṃ paṭipannattāpi suppaṭipanno.
rows.english: 92. Furthermore, the Community has entered on the good way because it has entered on the way according as instructed in the well-proclaimed Dhamma and Discipline (dhamma-vinaya), and because it has entered on the immaculate way.

rows.pali: Majjhimāya paṭipadāya antadvayamanupagamma paṭipannattā kāyavacīmanovaṅkakuṭilajimhadosappahānāya paṭipannattā ca ujuppaṭipannattā ca ujuppaṭipanno.
rows.english: It has entered on the straight way because it has entered on the way avoiding the two extremes and taking the middle course, and because it has entered on the way of the abandonment of the faults of bodily and verbal crookedness, tortuousness and warpedness.

rows.pali: Ñāyo vuccati nibbānaṃ.
rows.english: because Nibbāna is what is called “true”

rows.pali: Tadatthāya paṭipannattā ñāyappaṭipanno.
rows.english: It has entered on the true way and it has entered on the way with that as its aim.

rows.pali: Yathā paṭipannā sāmīcippaṭipannārahā honti, tathā paṭipannattā sāmīcippaṭipanno.
rows.english: It has entered on the proper way because it has entered on the way of those who are worthy of proper acts [of veneration].

rows.pali: 156.Yadidanti yāni imāni.
rows.english: 93. The word yadidaṃ (“that is to say”) = yāni imāni.

rows.pali: Cattāri purisayugānīti yugaḷavasena paṭhamamaggaṭṭho phalaṭṭhoti idamekaṃ yugaḷanti evaṃ cattāri purisayugaḷāni honti.
rows.english: The four pairs of men: taking them pairwise, the one who stands on the first path and the one who stands in the first fruition as one pair, in this way there are four pairs.

rows.pali: Aṭṭha purisapuggalāti purisapuggalavasena eko paṭhamamaggaṭṭho eko phalaṭṭhoti iminā nayena aṭṭheva purisapuggalā honti.
rows.english: The eight persons: taking them by persons, the one who stands on the first path as one and the one who stands in the first fruition as one, in this way there are eight persons.

rows.pali: Ettha ca purisoti vā puggaloti vā ekatthāni etāni padāni.
rows.english: And there in the compound purisa-puggala (persons) the words purisa and puggala have the same meaning,

rows.pali: Veneyyavasena panetaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: but it is expressed in this way to suit differing susceptibility to teaching.

rows.pali: Esa bhagavato sāvakasaṅghoti yānimāni yugavasena cattāri purisayugāni, pāṭiekkato aṭṭha purisapuggalā, esa bhagavato sāvakasaṅgho, āhuneyyotiādīsu ānetvā hunitabbanti āhunaṃ, dūratopi ānetvā sīlavantesu dātabbanti attho.
rows.english: This community of the Blessed One’s disciples: this community of the Blessed One’s disciples taken by pairs as the four pairs of men (purisa) and individually as the eight persons (purisa-puggala). 94.As to fit for gifts, etc.: what should be brought (ānetvā) and given (hunitabba) is a gift (āhuna—lit. “sacrifice”); the meaning is, what is to be brought even from far away and donated to the virtuous.

rows.pali: Catunnaṃ paccayānametamadhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: It is a term for the four requisites.

rows.pali: Taṃ āhunaṃ paṭiggahetuṃ yutto tassa mahapphalakaraṇatoti āhuneyyo.
rows.english: The Community is fit to receive that gift (sacrifice) because it makes it bear great fruit, thus it is “fit for gifts” (āhuneyya).

rows.pali: Atha vā dūratopi āgantvā sabbasāpateyyampi ettha hunitabbanti āhavanīyo.
rows.english: 95.Or alternatively, all kinds of property, even when the bringer comes (āgantvā) from far away, can be given (hunitabba) here, thus the Community “can be given to” (āhavanīya);

rows.pali: Sakkādīnampi vā āhavanaṃ arahatīti āhavanīyo.
rows.english: or it is fit to be given to by Sakka and others, thus it “can be given to.”

rows.pali: Yo cāyaṃ brāhmaṇānaṃ āhavanīyo nāma aggi, yattha hutaṃ mahapphalanti tesaṃ laddhi.
rows.english: And the brahmans’ fire is called “to be given (sacrificed) to” (āhavanīya), for they believe that what is sacrificed to it brings great fruit.

rows.pali: Sace hutassa mahapphalatāya āhavanīyo, saṅghova āhavanīyo.
rows.english: [220] But if something is to be sacrificed to for the sake of the great fruit brought by what is sacrificed to it, then surely the Community should be sacrificed to;

rows.pali: Saṅghe hutañhi mahapphalaṃ hoti.
rows.english: for what is sacrificed (given) to the Community has great fruit,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ nikāyantare āhavanīyoti padaṃ idha āhuneyyoti iminā padena atthato ekaṃ.
rows.english: And the words āhavanīya (“to be sacrificed to”), which is used in the schools,36 is the same in meaning as this word āhuneyya (“fit for gifts”) used here.

rows.pali: Byañjanato panettha kiñcimattameva nānaṃ.
rows.english: There is only the mere trifling difference of syllables.

rows.pali: Iti āhuneyyo.
rows.english: So it is “fit for gifts. ”

rows.pali: Pāhuneyyoti ettha pana pāhunaṃ vuccati disāvidisato āgatānaṃ piyamanāpānaṃ ñātimittānamatthāya sakkārena paṭiyattaṃ āgantukadānaṃ.
rows.english: 96.Fit for hospitality (pāhuneyya): “hospitality” (pāhuna) is what a donation to visitors is called, prepared with all honours for the sake of dear and beloved relatives and friends who have come from all quarters.

rows.pali: Tampi ṭhapetvā te tathārūpe pāhunake saṅghasseva dātuṃ yuttaṃ, saṅghova taṃ paṭiggahetuṃ yutto.
rows.english: But even more than to such objects of hospitality, it is fitting that it should be given also to the Community;

rows.pali: Saṅghasadiso hi pāhunako natthi.
rows.english: for there is no object of hospitality so fit to receive hospitality as the Community

rows.pali: Tathā hesa ekabuddhantare ca dissati, abbokiṇṇañca piyamanāpattakarehi dhammehi samannāgatoti.
rows.english: since it is encountered after an interval between Buddhas and possesses wholly endearing and lovable qualities.

rows.pali: Evaṃ pāhunamassa dātuṃ yuttaṃ pāhunañca paṭiggahetuṃ yuttoti pāhuneyyo.
rows.english: So it is “fit for hospitality” since the hospitality is fit to be given to it and it is fit to receive it.

rows.pali: Yesaṃ pana pāhavanīyoti pāḷi, tesaṃ yasmā saṅgho pubbakāramarahati, tasmā sabbapaṭhamaṃ ānetvā ettha hunitabbanti pāhavanīyo.
rows.english: But those who take the text to be pāhavanīya (“fit to be given hospitality to”) have it that the Community is worthy to be placed first and so what is to be given should first of all be brought here and given (sabba-Paṭhamaṃ Ānetvā ettha HUNitabbaṃ), and for that reason it is “fit to be given hospitality to” (pāhavanīya)

rows.pali: Sabbappakārena vā āhavanamarahatīti pāhavanīyo.
rows.english: or since it is worthy to be given to in all aspects (sabba-Pakārena ĀHAVANAṃ arahati), it is thus “fit to be given hospitality to” (pāhavanīya).

rows.pali: Svāyamidha teneva atthena pāhuneyyoti vuccati.
rows.english: And here this is called pāhuneyya in the same sense.

rows.pali: Dakkhiṇāti pana paralokaṃ saddahitvā dātabbadānaṃ vuccati.
rows.english: 97.”Offering” (dakkhiṇa) is what a gift is called that is to be given out of faith in the world to come.

rows.pali: Taṃ dakkhiṇaṃ arahati, dakkhiṇāya vā hito yasmā naṃ mahapphalakaraṇatāya visodhetīti dakkhiṇeyyo.
rows.english: The Community is worthy of that offering, or it is helpful to that offering because it purifies it by making it of great fruit, thus it is fit for offerings (dakkhiṇeyya).

rows.pali: Ubho hatthe sirasmiṃ patiṭṭhapetvā sabbalokena kayiramānaṃ añjalikammaṃ arahatīti añjalikaraṇīyo.
rows.english: It is worthy of being accorded by the whole world the reverential salutation (añjali-kamma) consisting in placing both hands [palms together] above the head, thus it is fit for reverential salutation (añjalikaraṇīya).

rows.pali: Anuttaraṃ puññakkhettaṃ lokassāti sabbalokassa asadisaṃ puññavirūhanaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: 98.As an incomparable field of merit for the world: as a place without equal in the world for growing merit;

rows.pali: Yathā hi rañño vā amaccassa vā sālīnaṃ vā yavānaṃ vā virūhanaṭṭhānaṃ rañño sālikkhettaṃ rañño yavakkhettanti vuccati, evaṃ saṅgho sabbalokassa puññānaṃ virūhanaṭṭhānaṃ.
rows.english: just as the place for growing the king’s or minister’s rice or corn is the king’s rice-field or the king’s corn-field, so the Community is the place for growing the whole world’s merit.

rows.pali: Saṅghaṃ nissāya hi lokassa nānappakārahitasukhasaṃvattanikāni puññāni virūhanti.
rows.english: For the world’s various kinds of merit leading to welfare and happiness grow with the Community as their support.

rows.pali: Tasmā saṅgho anuttaraṃ puññakkhettaṃ lokassāti.
rows.english: Therefore the Community is “an incomparable field of merit for the world.”

rows.pali: 157.Evaṃ suppaṭipannatādibhede saṅghaguṇe anussarato neva tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 99. As long as he recollects the special qualities of the Saṅgha in this way, classed as “having entered on the good way,” etc., [221] then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed,

rows.pali: Na dosa - pe - na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: or obsessed by hate, or obsessed by delusion;

rows.pali: Ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti saṅghaṃ ārabbhāti (a. ni. 6.10) purimanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by the Saṅgha” (A III 286). So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already described (§66), the jhāna factors arise in a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Saṅghaguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāraguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the Community’s special qualities, or else owing to his being occupied in recollecting special qualities of many sorts, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ saṅghaguṇānussaraṇavasena uppannattā saṅghānussaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access jhāna itself is known as “recollection of the Saṅgha” too because it arises with the recollection of the Community’s special qualities as the means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana saṅghānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu saṅghe sagāravo hoti sappatisso.
rows.english: 100.When a bhikkhu is devoted to this recollection of the Community, he is respectful and deferential towards the Community.

rows.pali: Saddhādivepullaṃ adhigacchati, pītipāmojjabahulo hoti, bhayabheravasaho, dukkhādhivāsanasamattho, saṅghena saṃvāsasaññaṃ paṭilabhati.
rows.english: He attains fullness of faith, and so on. He has much happiness and bliss. He conquers fear and dread. He is able to endure pain. He comes to feel as if he were living in the Community’s presence.

rows.pali: Saṅghaguṇānussatiyā ajjhāvutthañcassa sarīraṃ sannipatitasaṅghamiva uposathāgāraṃ pūjārahaṃ hoti, saṅghaguṇādhigamāya cittaṃ namati, vītikkamitabbavatthusamāyoge cassa sammukhā saṅghaṃ passato viya hirottappaṃ paccupaṭṭhāti, uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And his body, when the recollection of the Sangha’s special qualities dwells in it, becomes as worthy of veneration as an Uposatha house where the Community has met. His mind tends towards the attainment of the Community’s special qualities. When he encounters an opportunity for transgression, he has awareness of conscience and shame as vividly as if he were face to face with the Community. And if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ saṅghānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of the Community in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 158.Sīlānussatiṃ bhāvetukāmena pana rahogatena paṭisallīnena "aho vata me sīlāni akhaṇḍāni acchiddāni asabalāni akammāsāni bhujissāni viññuppasatthāni aparāmaṭṭhāni samādhisaṃvattanikānī"ti (a. ni. 6.10) evaṃ akhaṇḍatādiguṇavasena attano sīlāni anussaritabbāni.
rows.english: 101. One who wants to develop the recollection of virtue should go into solitary retreat and recollect his own different kinds of virtue in their special qualities of being untorn, etc., as follows: Indeed, my various kinds of virtue are “untorn, unrent, unblotched, unmottled, liberating, praised by the wise, not adhered to, and conducive to concentration” (A III 286).

rows.pali: Tāni ca gahaṭṭhena gahaṭṭhasīlāni, pabbajitena pabbajitasīlāni.
rows.english: And a layman should recollect them in the form of laymen’s virtue while one gone forth into homelessness should recollect them in the form of the virtue of those gone forth.

rows.pali: Gahaṭṭhasīlāni vā hontu pabbajitasīlāni vā, yesaṃ ādimhi vā ante vā ekampi na bhinnaṃ, tāni pariyante chinnasāṭako viya na khaṇḍānīti akhaṇḍāni.
rows.english: 102. Whether they are the virtues of laymen or of those gone forth, when no one of them is broken in the beginning or in the end, not being torn like a cloth ragged at the ends, then they are untorn.

rows.pali: Yesaṃ vemajjhe ekampi na bhinnaṃ, tāni majjhe vinividdhasāṭako viya na chiddānīti acchiddāni.
rows.english: When no one of them is broken in the middle, not being rent like a cloth that is punctured in the middle, then they are unrent.

rows.pali: Yesaṃ paṭipāṭiyā dve vā tīṇi vā na bhinnāni, tāni piṭṭhiyā vā kucchiyā vā uṭṭhitena dīghavaṭṭādisaṇṭhānena visabhāgavaṇṇena kāḷarattādīnaṃ aññatarasarīravaṇṇā gāvī viya na sabalānīti asabalāni.
rows.english: When they are not broken twice or thrice in succession, not being blotched like a cow whose body is some such colour as black or red with discrepant-coloured oblong or round patch appearing on her back or belly, then they are unblotched.

rows.pali: Yāni antarantarā na bhinnāni, tāni visabhāgabinduvicitrā gāvī viya na kammāsānīti akammāsāni.
rows.english: When they are not broken all over at intervals, not being mottled like a cow speckled with discrepant-coloured spots, then they are unmottled.

rows.pali: Avisesena vā sabbānipi sattavidhena methunasaṃyogena kodhupanāhādīhi ca pāpadhammehi anupahatattā akhaṇḍāni acchiddāni asabalāni akammāsāni.
rows.english: 103. Or in general they are untorn, unrent, unblotched, unmottled when they are undamaged by the seven bonds of sexuality (I.144) and by anger and enmity and the other evil things (see §59).

rows.pali: Tāniyeva taṇhādāsabyato mocetvā bhujissabhāvakaraṇena bhujissāni.
rows.english: 104. Those same virtues are liberating since they liberate by freeing from the slavery of craving.

rows.pali: Buddhādīhi viññūhi pasatthattā viññuppasatthāni.
rows.english: They are praised by the wise because they are praised by such wise men as Enlightened Ones.

rows.pali: Taṇhādiṭṭhīhi aparāmaṭṭhatāya kenaci vā ayaṃ te sīlesu dosoti evaṃ parāmaṭṭhuṃ asakkuṇeyyatāya aparāmaṭṭhāni.
rows.english: They are not adhered to (aparāmaṭṭha) since they are not adhered to (aparāmaṭṭhattā) with craving and [false] view, or because of the impossibility of misapprehending (parāmaṭṭhuṃ) that “There is this flaw in your virtues.”

rows.pali: Upacārasamādhiṃ appanāsamādhiṃ vā, atha vā pana maggasamādhiṃ phalasamādhiñcāpi saṃvattentīti samādhisaṃvattanikāni.
rows.english: They are conducive to concentration since they conduce to access concentration and absorption concentration, or to path concentration and fruition concentration.

rows.pali: 159.Evaṃ akhaṇḍatādiguṇavasena attano sīlāni anussarato nevassa tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 105. As long as he recollects his own virtues in their special qualities of being untorn, etc., in this way, then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed,

rows.pali: Na dosa - pe - na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: or obsessed by hate, or obsessed by delusion,

rows.pali: Ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti, sīlaṃ ārabbhāti purimanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by virtue” (A III 286). So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already described (§66), the jhāna factors arise in a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Sīlaguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāraguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the virtues’ special qualities, or owing to his being occupied in recollecting special qualities of many sorts, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ sīlaguṇānussaraṇavasena uppannattā sīlānussaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access jhāna itself is known as “recollection of virtue” too because it arises with the virtues’ special qualities as the means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana sīlānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu sikkhāya sagāravo hoti sabhāgavutti, paṭisanthāre appamatto, attānuvādādibhayavirahito, aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī, saddhādivepullaṃ adhigacchati, pītipāmojjabahulo hoti.
rows.english: 106. And when a bhikkhu is devoted to this recollection of virtue, he has respect for the training. He lives in communion [with his fellows in the life of purity]. He is sedulous in welcoming. He is devoid of the fear of self-reproach and so on. He sees fear in the slightest fault. He attains fullness of faith, and so on. He has much happiness and gladness.

rows.pali: Uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ sīlānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of virtue in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 160.Cāgānussatiṃ bhāvetukāmena pana pakatiyā cāgādhimuttena niccappavattadānasaṃvibhāgena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 107. One who wants to develop the recollection of generosity should be naturally devoted to generosity and the constant practice of giving and sharing.

rows.pali: Atha vā pana bhāvanaṃ ārabhantena ito dāni pabhuti sati paṭiggāhake antamaso ekālopamattampi dānaṃ adatvā na bhuñjissāmīti samādānaṃ katvā taṃdivasaṃ guṇavisiṭṭhesu paṭiggāhakesu yathāsatti yathābalaṃ dānaṃ datvā tattha nimittaṃ gaṇhitvā rahogatena paṭisallīnena "lābhā vata me suladdhaṃ vata me, yohaṃ maccheramalapariyuṭṭhitāya pajāya vigatamalamaccherena cetasā viharāmi muttacāgo payatapāṇi vossaggarato yācayogo dānasaṃvibhāgarato"ti evaṃ vigatamalamaccheratādiguṇavasena attano cāgo anussaritabbo.
rows.english: Or alternatively, if he is one who is starting the development of it, he should make the resolution: “From now on, when there is anyone present to receive, I shall not eat even a single mouthful without having given a gift.” And that very day he should give a gift by sharing according to his means and his ability with those who have distinguished qualities. When he has apprehended the sign in that, he should go into solitary retreat and recollect his own generosity in its special qualities of being free from the stain of avarice, etc., as follows: “It is gain for me, it is great gain for me, that in a generation obsessed by the stain of avarice I abide with my heart free from stain by avarice, and am freely generous and open-handed, that I delight in relinquishing, expect to be asked, and rejoice in giving and sharing” (A III 287).

rows.pali: Tattha lābhā vata meti mayhaṃ vata lābhā, ye ime "āyuṃ kho pana datvā āyussa bhāgī hoti dibbassa vā mānusassa vā" iti (a. ni. 5.37) ca, "dadaṃ piyo hoti bhajanti naṃ bahū" iti (a. ni. 5.34) ca, "dadamāno piyo hoti, sataṃ dhammaṃ anukkamaṃ" iti (a. ni. 5.35) ca evamādīhi nayehi bhagavatā dāyakassa lābhā saṃvaṇṇitā, te mayhaṃ avassaṃ bhāginoti adhippāyo.
rows.english: 108. Herein, it is gain for me: it is my gain, advantage. The intention is: I surely partake of those kinds of gain for a giver that have been commended by the Blessed One as follows: “A man who gives life [by giving food] shall have life either divine or human” (A III 42), and: “A giver is loved and frequented by many” (A III 40), and: “One who gives is ever loved, according to the wise man’s law” (A III 41), and so on.

rows.pali: Suladdhaṃ vata meti yaṃ mayā idaṃ sāsanaṃ manussattaṃ vā laddhaṃ, taṃ suladdhaṃ vata me.
rows.english: 109. It is great gain for me: it is great gain for me that this Dispensation, or the human state, has been gained by me.

rows.pali: Kasmā ?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Yohaṃ maccheramalapariyuṭṭhitāya pajāya - pe - dānasaṃvibhāgaratoti.
rows.english: Because of the fact that “I abide with my mind free from stain by avarice … and rejoice in giving and sharing.”

rows.pali: Tattha maccheramalapariyuṭṭhitāyāti maccheramalena abhibhūtāya.
rows.english: 110. Herein, obsessed by the stain of avarice is overwhelmed by the stain of avarice.

rows.pali: Pajāyāti pajāyanavasena sattā vuccanti.
rows.english: Generation: beings, so called owing to the fact of their being generated.

rows.pali: Tasmā attano sampattīnaṃ parasādhāraṇabhāvamasahanalakkhaṇena cittassa pabhassarabhāvadūsakānaṃ kaṇhadhammānaṃ aññatarena maccheramalena abhibhūtesu sattesūti ayamettha attho.
rows.english: So the meaning here is this: among beings who are overwhelmed by the stain of avarice, which is one of the dark states that corrupt the [natural] transparency of consciousness (see A I 10) and which has the characteristic of inability to bear sharing one’s own good fortune with others.

rows.pali: Vigatamalamaccherenāti aññesampi rāgadosādimalānañceva maccherassa ca vigatattā vigatamalamaccherena.
rows.english: 111. Free from stain by avarice because of being both free from avarice and from the other stains, greed, hate, and the rest.

rows.pali: Cetasā viharāmīti yathāvuttappakāracitto hutvā vasāmīti attho.
rows.english: I abide with my heart: I abide with my consciousness of the kind already stated, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Suttesu pana mahānāmasakkassa sotāpannassa sato nissayavihāraṃ pucchato nissayavihāravasena desitattā agāraṃ ajjhāvasāmīti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: But in the sutta, “I live the home life with my heart free” (A III 287; V 331), etc., is said because it was taught there as a [mental] abiding to depend on [constantly] to Mahānāma the Sakyan, who was a stream-enterer asking about an abiding to depend on.

rows.pali: Tattha abhibhavitvā vasāmīti attho.
rows.english: There the meaning is “I live overcoming …”

rows.pali: Muttacāgoti vissaṭṭhacāgo.
rows.english: 112. Freely generous: liberally generous.

rows.pali: Payatapāṇīti parisuddhahattho.
rows.english: Open-handed: with hands that are purified.

rows.pali: Sakkaccaṃ sahatthā deyyadhammaṃ dātuṃ sadā dhotahatthoyevāti vuttaṃ hoti.
rows.english: What is meant is: with hands that are always washed in order to give gifts carefully with one’s own hands.

rows.pali: Vossaggaratoti vossajjanaṃ vossaggo, pariccāgoti attho.
rows.english: That I delight in relinquishing: the act of relinquishing (vossajjana) is relinquishing (vossagga); the meaning is, giving up.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ vossagge satatābhiyogavasena ratoti vossaggarato.
rows.english: To delight in relinquishing is to delight in constant devotion to that relinquishing.

rows.pali: Yācayogoti yaṃ yaṃ pare yācanti, tassa tassa dānato yācanayogoti attho.
rows.english: Expect to be asked (yācayoga): accustomed to being asked (yācana-yogga) because of giving whatever others ask for, is the meaning.

rows.pali: Yājayogotipi pāṭho.
rows.english: Yājayoga is a reading,

rows.pali: Yajanasaṅkhātena yājena yuttoti attho.
rows.english: in which case the meaning is: devoted (yutta) to sacrifice (yāja), in other words, to sacrificing (yajana).

rows.pali: Dānasaṃvibhāgaratoti dāne ca saṃvibhāge ca rato.
rows.english: And rejoice in sharing:

rows.pali: Ahañhi dānañca demi, attanā paribhuñjitabbatopi ca saṃvibhāgaṃ karomi, ettheva casmi ubhaye ratoti evaṃ anussaratīti attho.
rows.english: the meaning is, he recollects thus: “I give gifts and I share out what is to be used by myself, and I rejoice in both.”

rows.pali: 161.Tassevaṃ vigatamalamaccheratādiguṇavasena attano cāgaṃ anussarato neva tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 113. As long as he recollects his own generosity in its special qualities of freedom from stain by avarice, etc., in this way, then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed,

rows.pali: Na dosa - pe - na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: or obsessed by hate, or obsessed by delusion;

rows.pali: Ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti cāgaṃ ārabbhāti (a. ni. 5.10) purimanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by generosity” (A III 287). So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already described (§66), the jhāna factors arise in a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Cāgaguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāracāgaguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the generosity’s special qualities, or owing to his being occupied in recollecting the generosity’s special qualities of many sorts, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ cāgaguṇānussaraṇavasena uppannattā cāgānussaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access jhāna is known as “recollection of generosity” too because it arises with the generosity’s special qualities as the means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana cāgānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu bhiyyoso mattāya cāgādhimutto hoti, alobhajjhāsayo, mettāya anulomakārī, visārado, pītipāmojjabahulo, uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: 114. And when a bhikkhu is devoted to this recollection of generosity, he becomes ever more intent on generosity, his preference is for non-greed, he acts in conformity with loving-kindness, he is fearless. He has much happiness and gladness. And if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ cāgānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of generosity in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 162.Devatānussatiṃ bhāvetukāmena pana ariyamaggavasena samudāgatehi saddhādīhi guṇehi samannāgatena bhavitabbaṃ.
rows.english: 115. One who wants to develop the recollection of deities should possess the special qualities of faith, etc., evoked by means of the noble path, recollect his own special qualities of faith, etc., with deities standing as witnesses, as follows:

rows.pali: Tato rahogatena paṭisallīnena "santi devā cātumahārājikā, santi devā tāvatiṃsā, yāmā, tusitā, nimmānaratino, paranimmitavasavattino, santi devā brahmakāyikā, santi devā tatuttari, yathārūpāya saddhāya samannāgatā tā devatā ito cutā tattha upapannā, mayhampi tathārūpā saddhā saṃvijjati.
rows.english: and he should go into solitary retreat and “There are deities of the Realm of the Four Kings (devā cātummahārājikā), there are deities of the Realm of the Thirty-three (devā tāvatiṃsā), there are deities who are Gone to Divine Bliss (yāmā) … who are Contented (tusitā) … who Delight in Creating (nimmānarati) … who Wield Power Over Others’ Creations (paranimmitavasavatti), there are deities of Brahmā’s Retinue (brahmakāyikā), there are deities higher than that. And those deities were possessed of faith such that on dying here they were reborn there, and such faith is present in me too.

rows.pali: Yathārūpena sīlena.
rows.english: And those deities were possessed of virtue …

rows.pali: Yathārūpena sutena.
rows.english: of learning …

rows.pali: Yathārūpena cāgena.
rows.english: of generosity …

rows.pali: Yathārūpāya paññāya samannāgatā tā devatā ito cutā tattha upapannā, mayhampi tathārūpā paññā saṃvijjatī"ti (a. ni. 6.10) evaṃ devatā sakkhiṭṭhāne ṭhapetvā attano saddhādiguṇā anussaritabbā.
rows.english: of understanding such that when they died here they were reborn there, and such understanding is present in me too” (A III 287).

rows.pali: Sutte pana yasmiṃ mahānāma samaye ariyasāvako attano ca tāsañca devatānaṃ saddhañca sīlañca sutañca cāgañca paññañca anussarati, nevassa tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hotīti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 116. In the sutta, however, it is said: “On the occasion, Mahānāma, on which a noble disciple recollects the faith, the virtue, the learning, the generosity, and the understanding that are both his own and of those deities,” on that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed …” (A III 287).

rows.pali: Kiñcāpi vuttaṃ, atha kho taṃ sakkhiṭṭhāne ṭhapetabbadevatānaṃ attano saddhādīhi samānaguṇadīpanatthaṃ vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Although this is said, it should nevertheless be understood as said for the purpose of showing that the special qualities of faith, etc., in oneself are those in the deities, making the deities stand as witnesses.

rows.pali: Aṭṭhakathāyañhi devatā sakkhiṭṭhāne ṭhapetvā attano guṇe anussaratīti daḷhaṃ katvā vuttaṃ.
rows.english: For it is said definitely in the Commentary: “He recollects his own special qualities, making the deities stand as witnesses.”

rows.pali: 163.Tasmā pubbabhāge devatānaṃ guṇe anussaritvā aparabhāge attano saṃvijjamāne saddhādiguṇe anussarato cassa neva tasmiṃ samaye rāgapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 117. As long as in the prior stage he recollects the deities’ special qualities of faith, etc., and in the later stage he recollects the special qualities of faith, etc., existing in himself, then: “On that occasion his mind is not obsessed by greed,

rows.pali: Na dosa - pe - na mohapariyuṭṭhitaṃ cittaṃ hoti, ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti devatā ārabbhāti (a. ni. 6.10) purimanayeneva vikkhambhitanīvaraṇassa ekakkhaṇe jhānaṅgāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: or obsessed by hate, or obsessed by delusion, his mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by deities” (A III 288). So when he has suppressed the hindrances in the way already stated (§66), the jhāna factors arise in a single conscious moment.

rows.pali: Saddhādiguṇānaṃ pana gambhīratāya nānappakāraguṇānussaraṇādhimuttatāya vā appanaṃ appatvā upacārappattameva jhānaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But owing to the profundity of the special qualities of faith, etc., or owing to his being occupied in recollecting special qualities of many sorts, the jhāna is only access and does not reach absorption.

rows.pali: Tadetaṃ devatānaṃ guṇasadisasaddhādiguṇānussaraṇavasena devatānussaticceva saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: And that access jhāna itself is known as “recollection of deities” too because it arises with the deities special qualities as the means.

rows.pali: Imañca pana devatānussatiṃ anuyutto bhikkhu devatānaṃ piyo hoti manāpo, bhiyyoso mattāya saddhādivepullaṃ adhigacchati, pītipāmojjabahulo viharati.
rows.english: 118. And when a bhikkhu is devoted to this recollection of deities, he becomes dearly loved by deities. He obtains even greater fullness of faith. He has much happiness and gladness.

rows.pali: Uttari appaṭivijjhanto pana sugatiparāyano hoti.
rows.english: And if he penetrates no higher, he is at least headed for a happy destiny.

rows.pali: Idaṃ devatānussatiyaṃ vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section dealing with the recollection of deities in the detailed explanation.

rows.pali: 164.Yaṃ pana etāsaṃ vitthāradesanāyaṃ "ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti tathāgataṃ ārabbhā"tiādīni vatvā "ujugatacitto kho pana, mahānāma, ariyasāvako labhati atthavedaṃ, labhati dhammavedaṃ, labhati dhammūpasaṃhitaṃ pāmojjaṃ, pamuditassa pīti jāyatī"ti (a. ni. 6.10) vuttaṃ, tattha itipi so bhagavātiādīnaṃ atthaṃ nissāya uppannaṃ tuṭṭhiṃ sandhāya labhati atthavedanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 119. Now, in setting forth the detail of these recollections, after the words, “His mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by the Perfect One,” it is added: “When a noble disciple’s mind has rectitude, Mahānāma, the meaning inspires him, the law inspires him, and the application of the law makes him glad. When he is glad, happiness is born in him” (A III 285–88). Herein, the meaning inspires him should be understood as said of contentment inspired by the meaning beginning, “This Blessed One is such since he is …” (§2).

rows.pali: Pāḷiṃ nissāya uppannaṃ tuṭṭhiṃ sandhāya labhati dhammavedaṃ.
rows.english: The law inspires him is said of contentment inspired by the text.

rows.pali: Ubhayavasena labhati dhammūpasaṃhitaṃ pāmojjanti vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: The application of the law makes him glad is said of both (cf. M-a I 173).

rows.pali: Yañca devatānussatiyaṃ devatā ārabbhāti vuttaṃ, taṃ pubbabhāge devatā ārabbha pavattacittavasena devatāguṇasadise vā devatābhāvanipphādake guṇe ārabbha pavattacittavasena vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 120. And when in the case of the recollection of deities inspired by deities is said, this should be understood as said either of the consciousness that occurs in the prior stage inspired by deities or of the consciousness [that occurs in the later stage] inspired by the special qualities that are similar to those of the deities and are productive of the deities’ state (cf. §117).

rows.pali: 165.Imā pana cha anussatiyo ariyasāvakānaññeva ijjhanti.
rows.english: 121. These six recollections succeed only in noble disciples.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ hi buddhadhammasaṅghaguṇā pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: For the special qualities of the Enlightened One, the Law, and the Community, are evident to them;

rows.pali: Te ca akhaṇḍatādiguṇehi sīlehi, vigatamalamaccherena cāgena, mahānubhāvānaṃ devatānaṃ guṇasadisehi saddhādiguṇehi samannāgatā.
rows.english: and they possess the virtue with the special qualities of untornness, etc., the generosity that is free from stain by avarice, and the special qualities of faith, etc., similar to those of deities.

rows.pali: Mahānāmasutte (a. ni. 6.10) ca sotāpannassa nissayavihāraṃ puṭṭhena bhagavatā sotāpannassa nissayavihāradassanatthameva etā vitthārato kathitā.
rows.english: 122. And in the Mahānāma Sutta (A III 285 f.) they are expounded in detail by the Blessed One in order to show a stream-winner an abiding to depend upon when he asked for one.

rows.pali: Gedhasuttepi "idha, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako tathāgataṃ anussarati, itipi so bhagavā - pe - ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti nikkhantaṃ muttaṃ vuṭṭhitaṃ gedhamhā.
rows.english: 123. Also in the Gedha Sutta : “Here, bhikkhus, a noble disciple recollects the Perfect One in this way: That Blessed One is such since he is accomplished … His mind has rectitude on that occasion. He has renounced, got free from, emerged from cupidity.

rows.pali: Gedhoti kho, bhikkhave, pañcannetaṃ kāmaguṇānamadhivacanaṃ.
rows.english: Cupidity, bhikkhus, is a term for the five cords of sense desire.

rows.pali: Idampi kho, bhikkhave, ārammaṇaṃ karitvā evamidhekacce sattā visujjhantī"ti (a. ni. 6.25) evaṃ ariyasāvakassa anussativasena cittaṃ visodhetvā uttari paramatthavisuddhiadhigamatthāya kathitā.
rows.english: Some beings gain purity here by making this [recollection] their prop” (A III 312). they are expounded in order that a noble disciple should purify his consciousness by means of the recollections and so attain further purification in the ultimate sense thus (see above).

rows.pali: Āyasmatā mahākaccānena desite sambādhokāsasuttepi "acchariyaṃ, āvuso, abbhutaṃ, āvuso, yāvañcidaṃ tena bhagavatā jānatā passatā arahatā sammāsambuddhena sambādhe okāsādhigamo anubuddho sattānaṃ visuddhiyā - pe - nibbānassa sacchikiriyāya yadidaṃ cha anussatiṭṭhānāni.
rows.english: 124. And in the Sambādhokāsa Sutta taught by the venerable Mahā-Kaccāna they are expounded as the realization of the wide-open through the susceptibility of purification that exists in the ultimate sense only in a noble disciple thus: “It is wonderful, friends, it is marvellous how the realization of the wide-open in the crowded [house life] has been discovered by the Blessed One who knows and sees, accomplished and fully enlightened, for the purification of beings, [for the surmounting of sorrow and lamentation, for the ending of pain and grief, for the attainment of the true way], for the realization of Nibbāna, that is to say, the six stations of recollection.

rows.pali: Katamāni cha?
rows.english: What six?

rows.pali: Idhāvuso, ariyasāvako tathāgataṃ anussarati - pe - evamidhekacce sattā visuddhidhammā bhavantī"ti (a. ni. 6.26) evaṃ ariyasāvakasseva paramatthavisuddhidhammatāya okāsādhigamavasena kathitā.
rows.english: Here, friends, a noble disciple recollects the Perfect One … Some beings are susceptible to purification in this way” (A III 314–15).

rows.pali: Uposathasuttepi "kathañca, visākhe, ariyūposatho hoti?
rows.english: 125. Also in the Uposatha Sutta: “And what is the Noble Ones’ Uposatha, Visākhā?

rows.pali: Upakkiliṭṭhassa, visākhe, cittassa upakkamena pariyodapanā hoti.
rows.english: It is the gradual cleansing of the mind still sullied by imperfections.

rows.pali: Kathañca, visākhe, upakkiliṭṭhassa cittassa upakkamena pariyodapanā hoti?
rows.english: And what is the gradual cleansing of the mind still sullied by imperfections?

rows.pali: Idha, visākhe, ariyasāvako tathāgataṃ anussaratī"ti (a. ni. 3.71) evaṃ ariyasāvakasseva uposathaṃ upavasato cittavisodhanakammaṭṭhānavasena uposathassa mahapphalabhāvadassanatthaṃ kathitā.
rows.english: Here, Visākhā, a noble disciple recollects the Perfect One …” (A I 206–11). they are expounded in order to show the greatness of the fruit of the Uposatha, as a mind-purifying meditation subject for a noble disciple who is observing the Uposatha.

rows.pali: Ekādasanipātepi "saddho kho, mahānāma, ārādhako hoti, no assaddho.
rows.english: 126. And in the Book of Elevens, (A V 328): “One who has faith is successful, Mahānāma, not one who has no faith.

rows.pali: Āraddhavīriyo, upaṭṭhitasati, samāhito, paññavā, mahānāma, ārādhako hoti, no duppañño.
rows.english: One who is energetic … One whose mindfulness is established … One who is concentrated … One who has understanding is successful, Mahānāma, not one who has no understanding.

rows.pali: Imesu kho tvaṃ, mahānāma, pañcasu dhammesu patiṭṭhāya cha dhamme uttari bhāveyyāsi.
rows.english: Having established yourself in these five things, Mahānāma, you should develop six things.

rows.pali: Idha tvaṃ, mahānāma, tathāgataṃ anussareyyāsi itipi so bhagavā"ti (a. ni. 11.11) evaṃ ariyasāvakasseva "tesaṃ no, bhante, nānāvihārena viharataṃ kenassa vihārena viharitabba"nti pucchato vihāradassanatthaṃ kathitā.
rows.english: Here, Mahānāma, you should recollect the Perfect One: That Blessed One is such since …” (A V 329–32). when a noble disciple has asked, “Venerable sir, in what way should we abide who abide in various ways?" they are expounded to him in order to show the way of abiding in this way (see above).

rows.pali: 166.Evaṃ santepi parisuddhasīlādiguṇasamannāgatena puthujjanenāpi manasi kātabbā.
rows.english: 127. Still, though this is so, they can be brought to mind by an ordinary man too, if he possesses the special qualities of purified virtue, and the rest.

rows.pali: Anussavavasenāpi hi buddhādīnaṃ guṇe anussarato cittaṃ pasīdatiyeva.
rows.english: For when he is recollecting the special qualities of the Buddha, etc., even only according to hearsay, his consciousness settles down,

rows.pali: Yassānubhāvena nīvaraṇāni vikkhambhetvā uḷārapāmojjo vipassanaṃ ārabhitvā arahattaṃyeva sacchikareyya kaṭaandhakāravāsī phussadevatthero viya.
rows.english: by virtue of which the hindrances are suppressed. In his supreme gladness he initiates insight, and he even attains to Arahantship, like the Elder Phussadeva who dwelt at Kaṭakandhakāra.

rows.pali: So kirāyasmā mārena nimmitaṃ buddharūpaṃ disvā "ayaṃ tāva sarāgadosamoho evaṃ sobhati, kathaṃ nu kho bhagavā na sobhati, so hi sabbaso vītarāgadosamoho"ti buddhārammaṇaṃ pītiṃ paṭilabhitvā vipassanaṃ vaḍḍhetvā arahattaṃ pāpuṇīti.
rows.english: 128. That venerable one, it seems, saw a figure of the Enlightened One created by Māra. He thought, “How good this appears despite its having greed, hate and delusion! What can the Blessed One’s goodness have been like? For he was quite without greed, hate and delusion! ” He acquired happiness with the Blessed One as object, and by augmenting his insight he reached Arahantship.

rows.pali: 164.Yaṃ pana etāsaṃ vitthāradesanāyaṃ "ujugatamevassa tasmiṃ samaye cittaṃ hoti tathāgataṃ ārabbhā"tiādīni vatvā "ujugatacitto kho pana, mahānāma, ariyasāvako labhati atthavedaṃ, labhati dhammavedaṃ, labhati dhammūpasaṃhitaṃ pāmojjaṃ, pamuditassa pīti jāyatī"ti (a. ni. 6.10) vuttaṃ, tattha itipi so bhagavātiādīnaṃ atthaṃ nissāya uppannaṃ tuṭṭhiṃ sandhāya labhati atthavedanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: 119. Now, in setting forth the detail of these recollections, after the words, “His mind has rectitude on that occasion, being inspired by the Perfect One,” it is added: “When a noble disciple’s mind has rectitude, Mahānāma, the meaning inspires him, the law inspires him, and the application of the law makes him glad. When he is glad, happiness is born in him” (A III 285–88). Herein, the meaning inspires him should be understood as said of contentment inspired by the meaning beginning, “This Blessed One is such since he is …” (§2).

rows.pali: Pāḷiṃ nissāya uppannaṃ tuṭṭhiṃ sandhāya labhati dhammavedaṃ.
rows.english: The law inspires him is said of contentment inspired by the text.

rows.pali: Ubhayavasena labhati dhammūpasaṃhitaṃ pāmojjanti vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: The application of the law makes him glad is said of both (cf. M-a I 173).

rows.pali: Yañca devatānussatiyaṃ devatā ārabbhāti vuttaṃ, taṃ pubbabhāge devatā ārabbha pavattacittavasena devatāguṇasadise vā devatābhāvanipphādake guṇe ārabbha pavattacittavasena vuttanti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 120. And when in the case of the recollection of deities inspired by deities is said, this should be understood as said either of the consciousness that occurs in the prior stage inspired by deities or of the consciousness [that occurs in the later stage] inspired by the special qualities that are similar to those of the deities and are productive of the deities’ state (cf. §117).

rows.pali: 165.Imā pana cha anussatiyo ariyasāvakānaññeva ijjhanti.
rows.english: 121. These six recollections succeed only in noble disciples.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ hi buddhadhammasaṅghaguṇā pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: For the special qualities of the Enlightened One, the Law, and the Community, are evident to them;

rows.pali: Te ca akhaṇḍatādiguṇehi sīlehi, vigatamalamaccherena cāgena, mahānubhāvānaṃ devatānaṃ guṇasadisehi saddhādiguṇehi samannāgatā.
rows.english: and they possess the virtue with the special qualities of untornness, etc., the generosity that is free from stain by avarice, and the special qualities of faith, etc., similar to those of deities.

rows.pali: Mahānāmasutte (a. ni. 6.10) ca sotāpannassa nissayavihāraṃ puṭṭhena bhagavatā sotāpannassa nissayavihāradassanatthameva etā vitthārato kathitā.
rows.english: 122. And in the Mahānāma Sutta (A III 285 f.) they are expounded in detail by the Blessed One in order to show a stream-winner an abiding to depend upon when he asked for one.

rows.english: 125. Also in the Uposatha Sutta: “And what is the Noble Ones’ Uposatha, Visākhā?

rows.pali: Upakkiliṭṭhassa, visākhe, cittassa upakkamena pariyodapanā hoti.
rows.english: It is the gradual cleansing of the mind still sullied by imperfections.

rows.english: And what is the gradual cleansing of the mind still sullied by imperfections?

rows.english: 126. And in the Book of Elevens, (A V 328): “One who has faith is successful, Mahānāma, not one who has no faith.

rows.english: One who is energetic … One whose mindfulness is established … One who is concentrated … One who has understanding is successful, Mahānāma, not one who has no understanding.
